---
title: "Quest 16: Closures - Quick Function Tools"
subtitle: "Use Inline Anonymous Functions"
format:
  live-html:
    code-tools: true
---

::: {.quest-badge}
⚡ QUEST 16 | Difficulty: Beginner | Time: 9 minutes
:::

::: {.tip-box}
**💻 Practice Options:**
- 🦀 [Open Rust Playground](https://play.rust-lang.org/)
- 📓 [Open Notebook](../jupyterlite/content/16-lambda-functions.ipynb)
- 📥 [Download Notebook](../files/lessons/16-lambda-functions.ipynb)
:::

## 📖 Introduction
Rust uses **closures** for compact function behavior.
Start small, then chain operations when you feel ready.

## 💡 Example

```rust
fn main() {
    let add_one = |x: i32| x + 1;
    println!("{}", add_one(9));
}
```

Closures are useful with iterators:

```rust
fn main() {
    let nums = vec![1, 2, 3, 4];
    let doubled: Vec<i32> = nums.iter().map(|n| n * 2).collect();
    println!("{:?}", doubled);
}
```

## 🎯 Gentle Challenge
Use a closure to square values in a vector.
Print the original vector and the squared vector.

## ✅ One Possible Solution

```rust
fn main() {
    let nums = vec![2, 3, 4];
    let squares: Vec<i32> = nums.iter().map(|n| n * n).collect();
    println!("{:?}", squares);
}
```

::: {.quest-complete}
### ✨ Quest 16 Complete
You built and used closures with iterators.
Next: [Quest 17: Higher-Order Functions](17-higher-order-functions.qmd)
:::
